title: TPU v1 · Specialized verification: CDC, low power, X propagation, registers, and connectivity—the five fatal defects ordinary simulation misses
oneLine: Correct matrix multiplication does not make a usable chip. Signals crossing the core, PCIe, and DDR3 domains can fail intermittently through metastability; an idle PE block can lose state from one-cycle power sequencing errors; reset-time X values may be hidden by optimistic RTL semantics until gate level; a wrong CSR reset value can misconfigure software at boot; and an unconnected pin may never be exercised. These five risks require dedicated CDC, UPF low-power, X-propagation, RAL register, and connectivity checks.

legend.name: Five specialized checks (outside ordinary functional simulation)
legend.desc: These are not ordinary stimulus-and-output tests. Each uses dedicated tools and methodology for a structural risk: CDC, UPF/power intent, unknown-value propagation, CSR registers, and connectivity. Standard RTL simulation either masks the behavior—such as optimistically resolving X to 0/1—or does not model it, such as metastability and power domains. Each therefore needs its own plan and signoff.

legend.name: ① Clock-domain crossing (CDC) verification
legend.desc: TPU spans at least the PCIe host, 700 MHz array-core, and off-chip DDR3 interface domains. Single-bit crossings need synchronizers; multibit transfers need Gray coding, handshakes, or asynchronous FIFOs. CDC analysis first checks structures, reconvergence, and reset-domain crossings (RDC), then simulation injects metastability/random delay before synchronizers to verify downstream robustness.

legend.name: ② Low-power verification (UPF)
legend.desc: TPU's full-chip power is 28–40 W, making clock gating or power-down of idle PE/buffer blocks a natural technique. UPF defines switchable domains, isolation values, retention state, and the required sequence: isolate → power off → power on → release isolation → restore. UPF-aware simulation verifies state preservation, isolation, recovery, and freedom from X throughout transitions.

legend.name: ③ X-propagation verification
legend.desc: Pre-reset flip-flops, uninitialized RAM such as the 24 MiB UB and 4 MiB accumulator memory, and multiple-driver conflicts create X values. RTL simulation can be optimistic: if/ternary logic may resolve an X to a deterministic branch and hide the bug until gate level. Pessimistic X-prop verifies complete reset, write-before-read behavior, and prevention of unknowns entering the datapath.

legend.name: ④ CSR register verification (RAL)
legend.desc: The host configures many control/status registers over PCIe. A Register Abstraction Layer automatically checks each specified reset value, read/write field through write-readback, read-only behavior, W1C (write 1 to clear), and reserved bits. Ordinary functional tests rarely sweep every bit, yet one incorrect reset attribute can derail software immediately after boot.

legend.name: ⑤ Connectivity verification
legend.desc: After top-level integration, point-to-point connections from chip pins/IO to internal ports and static power, scan, JTAG, bypass, or test signals must be complete, correctly polarized, and not swapped. Formal or dedicated connectivity tools compare every source pin → destination port without relying on functional stimulus; an unused bypass path might otherwise remain untested until a critical mode.

steps.cap: ① CDC: analyze PCIe ↔ core ↔ DDR3 boundaries. Confirm every crossing uses a valid synchronizer, Gray code, or handshake, then inject metastability to test robustness against occasional resampling.
② Low power: run idle-PE gating/power-down with UPF. Verify isolation clamps, retention, and power sequencing, with no state loss or X after recovery.
③ X propagation: use pessimistic semantics to confirm reset clears PE-array state and that reading the 24 MiB UB / 4 MiB accumulator before write cannot leak X into the datapath.
④ CSR: use RAL to sweep reset defaults, write-readback, read-only, and W1C behavior across all control/status registers, catching boot-default defects.
⑤ Connectivity: point-to-point compare every top-level pin→port connection for swaps and omissions. Passing all five specialized checks → specialized signoff → synthesis ⑦.

before: List specialized verification as independent plan items: CDC, low power, X, registers, and connectivity use different tools, criteria, and evidence. Define the target paths/domains/registers, tool, and pass gate for each before work begins. Omitting one leaves a distinct silicon risk.

Establish golden intent for CDC and low power: CDC needs an explicit domain inventory—PCIe, 700 MHz core, and DDR3—and asynchronous-clock constraints in SDC. Low power needs UPF definitions of domains, isolation, retention, and sequencing. Verification is meaningful only if these intent files are correct.

Define the register specification as the CSR Reference Model: use a machine-readable table of every address, reset value, and RO/RW/W1C/reserved-field attribute to generate the RAL model. A stale or incorrect table creates false pass results.

Specify X semantics upfront: enable pessimistic X behavior or a dedicated X-prop tool rather than relying on default optimistic RTL. Completion requires no X on state/control after reset and no UB/accumulator read before write.

Define the connectivity checklist and method: enumerate point-to-point IO, power, scan, JTAG, bypass, and test connections. Use formal/dedicated source→destination checking rather than stimulus, since an omitted static path may never activate in functional regression.
during: Rule: CDC needs structural analysis and metastability testing: a recognized synchronizer alone does not prove downstream logic tolerates occasional resampling. Analyze synchronizers, Gray code, handshakes, reconvergence, and RDC, then inject metastability/random delay across PCIe↔core↔DDR3 boundaries.

Low power: verify transition order and recovery, not only steady states: defects cluster at power edges. Missing isolation before power-off releases X; failed retention loses configuration; wrong isolation-release/restore order corrupts FSM state. UPF-aware tests must target every transition.

X propagation: reject optimistic masking: an RTL pass followed by gate-level failure frequently indicates hidden X. X-prop reveals unreset FSM state, read-before-write in the 24 MiB UB / 4 MiB accumulator memory, and unisolated power crossings. Fix the root cause—reset, initialization, or isolation—rather than forcing X to 0 in simulation.

CSR: automate with RAL rather than hand-writing every register test: generate RAL from the specification and run reset-value, write-readback, bit-bash, and alias sequences across tens or hundreds of registers. Add directed tests only for side effects such as a write that launches hardware activity.

Connectivity: use point-to-point formal comparison, not functional stimulus: exhaustively compare each source→destination for correct endpoint and polarity and detect omissions or swaps. This directly covers static paths that no practical functional regression guarantees.

Scope note: CDC, UPF low power, X-prop, UVM RAL, and connectivity are standard large-ASIC verification disciplines. TPU v1's multiple domains, idle-block gating, large RAMs, and CSR interface are valid targets, but specific tools, metastability parameters, and register lists are undisclosed.

result.label: Five specialized disciplines
result.value: CDC · low power (UPF) · X propagation · CSR (RAL) · connectivity

result.label: CDC criterion
result.value: 0 structural crossing violations + no intermittent functional error after metastability injection

result.label: Low-power criterion
result.value: Correct power-off/retention/isolation/recovery sequence; no state loss or X

result.label: X semantics (illustrative)
result.value: Pessimistic X / X-prop enabled; no X after reset; no UB/accumulator X from read-before-write

result.label: CSR sweep (illustrative)
result.value: RAL: reset-value check + write-readback + RO/W1C attributes (automatic)

after: Converge and sign off all five independently: require no unwaived CDC violations, correct UPF transition simulations, no illegal X, full RAL pass, and a clean connectivity report. Each supplies separate evidence; none is optional.

Rerun the affected specialized regression after every change: crossing edits trigger CDC, UPF/domain edits trigger low-power tests, register changes regenerate and rerun RAL, and top-level wiring changes rerun connectivity. Keep these alongside functional regression in continuous verification.

Keep intent files synchronized with RTL: clock constraints, UPF, register specification, and connectivity map are golden intent. Review them after RTL revisions; verifying against stale intent creates false signoff.

Hand constraints, power, and CDC information downstream: CDC conclusions, UPF power intent, register and connectivity data continue into logic synthesis ⑦ and physical implementation. Clock trees, power networks, and scan insertion must preserve the assumptions under which verification passed.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (public TPU v1 structures include multiple clock-domain interfaces, idle-block gating, 24 MiB UB / 4 MiB accumulator memory, and PCIe CISC/register interfaces; the exact tools, parameters, and environments for CDC, UPF low power, X propagation, RAL, and connectivity are standard verification-practice examples because TPU has not published specialized-verification details)
